food_subgroup: Spiced tomato-cream chicken curry with steamed rice

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Butter Chicken. Nearest parent is a spiced chicken curry. A new FoodOn concept is recommended.
ontology.foodex2_description: Meat-based curry dishes — chicken in spiced tomato cream sauce (closest parent)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Butter Chicken with rice weighs approximately 450g and consists of 150g of tandoor-marinated chicken pieces in a rich, mildly spiced tomato-cream-butter sauce served over 300g of steamed white rice.

nutrient_source.title: Butter Chicken — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, Food Code F11020052
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), Hyderabad
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: The database cr_id F11020052 maps to a chicken curry preparation in HPB. ICMR-NIN murgh makhani values used as primary source. Combined dish value includes rice.

gi_source: University of Sydney GI Database — white jasmine rice (main GI contributor); butter chicken sauce has negligible GI.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium approximately 1701mg per serving (450g) — about 74% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit; the creamy sauce contains salt, butter, and cream as significant contributors.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced salt in the butter chicken preparation; the cream and butter content is cardiovascularly significant but the sodium level is the primary hypertension concern.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Jasmine rice base has a moderate-high GI (~58); the cream and fat content in the sauce slows gastric emptying, moderately blunting the glycaemic response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Diabetic patients should reduce the rice portion and focus on the chicken; the fat content of the sauce provides some glycaemic modulation, making this a moderate choice compared to plain rice dishes.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Butter and cream are sources of saturated fat and arachidonic acid; however, turmeric and garam masala in the sauce provide anti-inflammatory curcumin.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should consume Butter Chicken in moderation; the cream and butter add saturated fat that can promote inflammation, but the anti-inflammatory spice profile partially offsets this.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Butter Chicken (Murgh Makhani) is a popular North Indian dish of tandoor-cooked chicken pieces simmered in a mildly spiced, velvety sauce made from puréed tomatoes, cream, butter, cashew paste, ginger, garlic, and aromatic spices including garam masala and kasuri methi (dried fenugreek). The sauce has a characteristically rich, orange-red colour and a mild, creamy sweetness balanced by warm spices. It was reportedly invented at a Delhi restaurant in the 1950s and is now one of the most widely consumed Indian dishes globally, available at every Indian restaurant in Singapore.
common_user_queries: how many calories in butter chicken
can a diabetic eat butter chicken
butter chicken vs tikka masala difference

confidence_reason: Exact match sourced from ICMR-NIN for murgh makhani preparation; adapted for the combined dish with rice. DB food code F11020052 confirmed as chicken curry entry in HPB.
notes: Contains dairy (cream, butter) — not suitable for lactose-intolerant or dairy-free patients. The restaurant versions vary significantly in cream and butter quantity; home-cooked versions may have less fat. Cashew nut allergen is a significant concern.
